evals.name: new-feature-brainstorm
evals.prompt: Brainstorm adding real-time collaboration to our document editor. Multiple users should be able to edit the same document simultaneously, like Google Docs.
evals.expected_output: A structured exploration through all phases ending with a design document saved to docs/plans/, with explicit user approval gates at each stage
expectations: Uses the AskUserQuestion tool for all questions rather than embedding questions in plain text output
Begins with Phase 1 context gathering by reviewing relevant codebase files and asking 2-3 initial questions about current state
Phase 2 explores non-functional requirements including performance, scalability, reliability, and security
Phase 2 actively hunts for edge cases such as concurrent edits, network failures, and conflict resolution
Phase 4 presents 2-3 distinct architectural approaches with trade-offs, risks, and a recommendation
Phase 5 performs explicit gap analysis checking for blind spots, open questions, and unvalidated assumptions
Phase 6 produces a design document saved to docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md
Phase 6 presents the design section by section using AskUserQuestion for approval on each section
anti_expectations: Does not write any implementation code, scaffolding, or pseudocode during the brainstorming session
Does not skip directly to proposing a solution without first exploring the problem space in Phases 1-2
Does not embed questions in plain text output instead of using the AskUserQuestion tool
Does not proceed to implementation before receiving explicit user approval of the design

evals.name: minor-change-depth-scaling
evals.prompt: Think through adding a dark mode toggle to our settings page. We already have a theme context provider, just need the UI toggle and to persist the preference.
evals.expected_output: A scaled-down brainstorm appropriate for a minor change: brief context gathering, quick risk check, short design summary rather than exhaustive multi-phase exploration
expectations: Scales depth appropriately to a minor change rather than running full-depth exploration on all phases
Still performs some form of context gathering even though the problem seems simple
Uses the AskUserQuestion tool for questions rather than embedding them in plain text
Considers at least one non-functional concern (e.g., persistence mechanism, accessibility, SSR compatibility)
Identifies edge cases even for this minor feature (e.g., system preference changes, stored preference conflicts)
Produces a design document even if brief, saved to docs/plans/
anti_expectations: Does not skip brainstorming entirely just because the problem seems simple
Does not jump straight to writing implementation code or providing code snippets
Does not produce an exhaustive multi-page design document disproportionate to the problem size

evals.name: code-level-refactoring-brainstorm
evals.prompt: Help me think through refactoring our payment processing module. It's a 2000-line file handling Stripe, PayPal, and bank transfers all in one class. Error handling is inconsistent and adding new payment methods is painful.
evals.expected_output: Exploration that reads and analyzes existing code as part of context gathering, identifies architectural challenges around boundaries and interfaces, and produces a design focused on structural changes without prescribing specific code
expectations: Reads and analyzes existing code files during Phase 1 context gathering since the problem is code-level
Uses AskUserQuestion tool for all interactive questions
Explores the problem from multiple perspectives including the developer maintaining it, the system under load, and failure/recovery modes
Phase 3 identifies architectural challenges around component boundaries, interfaces, and contracts between payment providers
Phase 4 presents 2-3 distinct refactoring approaches with trade-offs (e.g., strategy pattern vs. microservices vs. plugin architecture)
Design stays at the architectural level describing what changes structurally rather than prescribing specific code changes
Phase 7 asks for explicit design approval using AskUserQuestion before any implementation begins
anti_expectations: Does not start refactoring or writing code during the brainstorming session
Does not produce pseudocode or code-level implementation details in the design document
Does not analyze only the happy path while ignoring error handling and failure modes
Does not treat edge cases like partial payment failures or provider outages as afterthoughts
